config.title: Exposure, Vulnerability, and Adaptation to Climate Hazards
config.abstract: Extreme weather events, such as floods, droughts, storms, and heatwaves occur more often and are stronger due to climate change and impact more than half the world’s population. Almost a third of those affected are also in highly vulnerable conditions and must adapt to a changing climate.
config.citation: Benschop, T, Groves, D, Lønborg, J H and Rininsland, Æ. 2026. “Exposure, vulnerability and adaptation to climate hazards.” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by A F Pirlea, D Wadhwa, D Mahler, U Serajuddin, M Welch, A Thudt, and M Lambrechts. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
config.credits: Thijs Benschop, Jonas Helth Lønborg & David Groves
config.inputs: Jia Li, Esther Naikal, Minh Cong Nguyen
config.homeFactoidText: people are exposed and vulnerable to climate hazards
config.homeTagline: Climate change intensifies extreme weather events, exposing more than half the global population, a large share of which is also vulnerable and in need of adaptation.

content.text: Climate change affects people, livelihoods and the economy

Climate change affects natural and human systems as temperatures rise, rainfall patterns shift, and extreme weather events, such as heatwaves, floods, droughts, and storms, become more frequent and intense.[reference: IPCC2023] These changes disrupt ecosystems and economies alike. Prolonged droughts reduce agricultural yields,[reference: FAO1] floods destroy homes and infrastructure, and heat stress threatens health and productivity.[reference: ILO1]

These impacts are not felt equally. People in different locations face different levels of exposures to climate hazards. However, even when faced with the same hazard, some people are more likely to be adversely affected or unable to cope with its impacts, for instance due to living in poverty or lacking access to social protection.[reference: WB3]
Let’s see which climate hazards the world’s population is exposed to and what makes people vulnerable.
Exposure to climate hazards

We focus on four climate-related hazards: [emphasis: floods],[emphasis: ][emphasis: agricultural droughts], [emphasis: cyclones][emphasis: ]and [emphasis: heatwaves]. Not all places are equally exposed to them and the number of people living in affected areas differs substantially. Therefore, to estimate the number of people per country who are exposed, we need to overlay the geographical location of the hazards with the number of people living there.

The population exposed is defined as those people living in an area affected by a given climate-related hazard. An area is considered exposed if the probability that a hazard strikes in a given year is more than one percent. This translates into a[emphasis:  ]probability of slightly over 50 percent[footnote: To estimate the exposure, a 100-year return period is considered, which is the same as a 1 percent probability that the hazard occurs in a given year. The average life expectancy in the world is 72 years. An event with a probability of annual occurrence of 0.01 has a probability of 1-(1-0.01)^72=0.515 or 52% to occur in a period of 72 years. ] that a person faces the hazard over their life span. Weather events are considered hazards when their intensity is high enough to significantly impact lives, assets and livelihoods.[footnote:  To determine whether a climate-related hazard has a significant impact hazard-specific thresholds are used. For instance, for floods a threshold is used for inundation depth and for cyclones a threshold for wind speed is used.]

content.title: Population exposed to selected climate-related hazards
content.label: globally in 2024[reference: WB1]

content.text: Let’s look at the people exposed to [emphasis: floods],[emphasis: ][emphasis: agricultural droughts], [emphasis: cyclones][emphasis: ]and [emphasis: heatwaves].

[emphasis: Floods] occur because of heavy rain, overflowing rivers and rising sea levels. Climate change affects precipitation patterns, snow and ice melt as well as sea level rise leading to more frequent and heavier floods.[reference: UNEP1]

content.title: 775 million people are exposed to floods globally
content.subtitle: Area and share of population exposed to floods with inundation depth over 50 cm[footnote:  An area is defined as being exposed to floods if there is an annual probability of more than 1 percent to experience flooding with inundation depth exceeding 50 centimeters.]
content.source: Deltares, Fathom, Hill et al. [reference: WB2]
content.visdescription: Share of population exposed to floods and location of areas exposed to flood risk

scenes.text: [emphasis: Floods] can severely damage infrastructure and homes, disrupt the economy, and destroy crops and livestock. Impacts become particularly severe when inundation depth exceeds 50 centimeters.[reference: Rentschler] Areas shown in [emphasis: blue] indicate exposure above this level.

The [emphasis: location of the population exposed to ] [emphasis: floods] is closely tied to the location of rivers, land elevation and coastal proximity. For example, in The Netherlands more than a third of the population is exposed to [emphasis: floods] due to its many rivers and the low elevation.

Globally, [emphasis: 775 million people are exposed to] [emphasis: floods.] Half of them live in three populous countries: China, India and Bangladesh. In Bangladesh, one third of the population is exposed to [emphasis: floods].

content.text: [emphasis: Agricultural droughts] occur when soil moisture and water availability become insufficient to meet the needs of crops and livestock resulting in reduced agricultural production. This undermines food security and rural livelihoods. Climate change is intensifying these droughts by increasing their frequency, severity, and duration, placing growing pressure on already vulnerable farming systems.

content.title: Almost 1.4 billion people in rural areas are exposed to agricultural drought globally
content.subtitle: Areas and share of population exposed to agricultural drought events.[footnote:  An area is defined as being exposed to agricultural drought if at least once in the last 39 years more than 30 percent of cropland or pasture is lost due to drought.]
content.source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, Hill et al. [reference: WB2]
content.visdescription: Share of population exposed to agricultural drought and location of areas exposed to agricultural drought.

scenes.text: An [emphasis: area] is considered to be exposed to [emphasis: agricultural drought] if more than 30 percent of the cropland or pasture experienced severe drought in at least one year over the past four decades.[footnote: This is measured using the Agricultural Stress Index (ASI) developed by FAO.[reference: FAO2] [reference: VanHoolst] ] When this threshold is exceeded, agricultural production losses are expected. [emphasis: Land affected by agricultural droughts has more than doubled in the last 40 years.]
[emphasis: Agricultural drought] is most prevalent in arid and semi-arid regions. In Eswatini, Lesotho, Namibia, and Zimbabwe more than half the population is exposed to drought.

Almost 1.4 billion people are exposed to [emphasis: agricultural drought][reference: WB2] and droughts can halt or reverse progress in poverty reduction and food security. Beyond agriculture, prolonged droughts also contribute to environmental degradation, forced migration, and heightened social tensions.

content.text: [emphasis: ]

[emphasis: Cyclones] are large revolving storms that form over tropical oceans and are also known as hurricanes or typhoons.[footnote: Different names are used for tropical cyclones depending on their location. Over the Atlantic Ocean and East Pacific Ocean, tropical cyclones are commonly called hurricanes. Tropical cyclones that form in the West Pacific Ocean are referred to as typhoons. Those in the Indian Ocean and around Australia are called cyclones.] Climate change causes ocean water temperatures to rise, which causes cyclones to be stronger resulting in heavier rainfall.

content.title: Exposure to cyclones
content.subtitle: Area and share of population exposed to cyclones of category 2 or higher[footnote:  The strength of a cyclone is assessed on a scale from 1 to 5 using the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Wind Scale.[reference: NOAA1]]
content.source: STORM [reference: Bloemendaal1], Hill et al. [reference: WB2]
content.visdescription: Share of population exposed to cyclones and location of areas exposed to cyclones

scenes.text: Tropical [emphasis: cyclones] of category 2 or higher[footnote: Cyclones are measured on the Saffir Simpson scale, which classifies them into five categories (1–5) based on their maximum sustained wind speed. Cyclones with wind speeds over 154 km/h are classified as Category 2 and above and can cause extensive damage.] are associated with extensive damage to buildings and infrastructure.

The tropical [emphasis: cyclone] Melissa made landfall in Jamaica on October 28, 2025. It was the third most intense [emphasis: cyclone] in the Atlantic Ocean ever recorded, classified as a category 5 strength cyclone with wind speeds up to 300 km/h.[reference: WMO4] The damage to infrastructure and houses was enormous, but due to early warning systems, the number of casualties was limited at 50.[emphasis: ]

[emphasis: Cyclones] occur over warm tropical oceans exposing people in their proximity. In fact, in many Small Island Developing States (SIDS), the entire population is exposed. Thirty-six small island developing states in the Caribbean and Pacific Ocean have 100 percent of their population exposed to tropical [emphasis: cyclones].[footnote: These are the following small island developing states: Aruba, Anguilla, American Samoa, Antigua and Barbuda, Bonaire, Sint Eustatius and Saba, Bahamas, Saint Barthélemy, Bermuda, Barbados, Cuba, Curaçao, Cayman Islands, Dominica, Dominican Republic, Grenada, Guam, Haiti, Jamaica, Saint Kitts and Nevis, Saint Lucia, Saint Martin (French part), Northern Mariana Islands, Montserrat, Mauritius, Niue, Puerto Rico, Sint Maarten (Dutch part), Turks and Caicos Islands, Tonga, Taiwan, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, British Virgin Islands, United States Virgin Islands, Vanuatu, Wallis and Futuna, Samoa. In Fiji more than 99 percent of the population is exposed.]

content.text: [emphasis: Heatwaves] are prolonged periods of unusually high temperatures. Heatwaves can impact human health through an increase in the risk of dehydration, heat exhaustion and heatstroke, and worsen cardiovascular and respiratory diseases. This significantly raises mortality especially among older adults and people with pre-existing conditions.[reference: WHO1] Heatwaves can also reduce economic output, as labor productivity, particularly in outdoor activities such as agriculture, construction, and manufacturing, becomes more strenuous. Additionally, heatwaves increase demand for electricity, and puts additional stress on infrastructure, and healthcare systems.[reference: ILO1]

content.title: 40 percent of the global population is exposed to heatwaves
content.subtitle: Areas and share of population exposed to heatwaves[footnote:   A heatwave is defined as having 5-day average maximum Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature exceeding 33 degrees. ]
content.source: World Bank Climate Change Portal, Hill et al. [reference: WB2]
content.visdescription: Share of population exposed to heatwaves and location of areas exposed to heatwaves[emphasis: ]

scenes.text: The impact [emphasis: heatwaves] have on the human body also depends on other factors such as humidity and wind. Instead of using a simple temperature measure, we rely on a measure called the Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature (WBGT), as it better captures the effect of temperature on the human body. If the WBGT exceeds 33 degrees Celsius, it becomes difficult for healthy humans to maintain a normal body temperature, which leads to increased mortality and hospitalizations. The capacity to do physical work is also halved.[reference: WB2]

[emphasis: Heatwaves] are mostly prevalent in the tropics. More than three quarters of the global population exposed to heatwaves live in four populous countries in Asia: India, China, Pakistan, Bangladesh.

Globally, 40 percent of the population is exposed to [emphasis: heatwaves]. The share of people exposed to [emphasis: heatwaves] is especially high in countries around the Equator in Africa, South-East Asia and the Arabian peninsula.

content.text: If we overlay exposures to each of the four climate hazards with population density, 4.5 billion people or around 57 percent of the global population is exposed to at least one climate hazard. There is no country in the world with no people exposed to [emphasis: floods],[emphasis: ][emphasis: agricultural droughts], [emphasis: cyclones][emphasis: ]or [emphasis: heatwaves], but the share of people exposed varies from less than one to 100 percent of a country’s population.

content.title: 57 percent of global population exposed to climate-related hazards
content.subtitle: Share of population exposed to at least one climate-related hazard
content.visdescription: Share of population exposed to at least one climate hazard
content.textParagraph: The exposure to the climate hazards floods, agricultural droughts, cyclones and heatwaves depends on the geographical location of a country. In 44 countries the entire population is exposed to at least one climate hazard.
content.scale_title: Percentage of population exposed to climate-related hazards
content.scale_title_pop: Total number of people exposed to climate-hazard
content.scale_title_sha: Percentage of population exposed to climate effects
content.keyFactLabel: people are exposed to climate-related hazards
content.legend_title: Percentage of population exposed to climate effects

content.text: 2.8 billion people are highly vulnerable

Mapping climate-related hazards is only half the task; the other half is understanding people’s vulnerability to such climate hazards. The loss of income or damage to property caused by a heat wave, drought, or flood may be a small inconvenience for some but a long‑term livelihood shock for others. The effect of the climate-related hazard depends on people’s ability and capacity to cope, adapt and recover. In other words, climate risk is shaped not just by exposure, but also by vulnerability.

The World Bank has identified seven dimensions to assess a person’s general level of vulnerability.[footnote:  The seven dimensions of vulnerability are defined based on existing literature as well as data availability.] These dimensions are grouped into physical and economic vulnerability. People’s physical vulnerability is measured by their access to [emphasis: clean water, ]to [emphasis: electricity],[emphasis:  ]and to[emphasis:  services and markets]. Their economic vulnerability is measured by their [emphasis: income, ] (add link to SDG 1), their [emphasis: education], their access to [emphasis: social protection ]and access to financial services ([emphasis: financial inclusion]).[reference: WB2] Individuals are considered to live in highly vulnerable conditions, if they score below set thresholds on at least one of these seven dimensions. For vulnerability due to income, for instance, people are identified as living in highly vulnerable conditions if their income falls below the extreme poverty line of $3 a day.[reference: WB2]

content.title: People identified as living in highly vulnerable conditions
content.label: globally in 2024[reference: WB1]

content.text: More than 1 in 3 people globally are considered highly vulnerable in at least one dimension. Many people even experience vulnerability across several of these dimensions. Let’s look at how many people are vulnerable in each country and along which dimensions of vulnerability.[footnote:  We restrict ourselves to the six dimensions of the vulnerability that come from household surveys. The seventh dimension is the access to roads and markets, which is measured using spatial data. As it is not possible to combine the spatial data with the survey data, it is not possible to assess the overlap of these dimensions with the six others. However, the spatial vulnerability measure affects only 44 million out of the total number of 2.8 billion vulnerable worldwide.]

content.title: People can be in highly vulnerable conditions due to several different reasons
content.subtitle: Number and share of people vulnerable by dimension, 2024
content.keyFactLabel: people are in highly vulnerable conditions
content.label__vul_atleastone: Vulnerable population (in at least one dimension)

scenes.text: There are 1,000 squares in each column and each individual square represents 8 million people of the global population. More than a third of the[emphasis: ][emphasis: global population], or 2.8 billion people, are vulnerable in at least one dimension. Almost half of them are vulnerable along two or more dimensions. Lack of social protection is the most common source of vulnerability, with 20 percent of the global population facing it. Lack of financial inclusion, lack of education, and living in extreme poverty due to low income are the next leading causes of vulnerability.

The level of vulnerability ranges from 1.7 to 93 percent across regions. In [emphasis: North America], only 1.7 percent of the population are identified as being vulnerable. These are people living in extreme poverty (lack of income) or lacking basic education.

In [emphasis: Sub-Saharan Africa,] a staggering 94 percent of the population is vulnerable. Lack of social protection impacts almost 3 out of 4 people in the region and lack of access to electricity or extreme poverty affects almost half the population.
Select a country or region to see the share of population and number of people that are vulnerable and which dimensions of vulnerability are driving it.

content.text: 1.5 billion people are at high risk of climate-related hazards

When we combine exposure to climate-related hazards with vulnerabilities, we can identify people [emphasis: at high risk from climate-related hazards]. People are considered at high risk when they are exposed to any of the four climate-related hazards and are considered highly vulnerable.

content.title: At high risk of climate-related hazards
content.label: People globally are both exposed to climate-related hazards [emphasis: and][emphasis:  ]in highly vulnerable conditions.[footnote:  World Bank Scorecard Indicator.]

content.title: The number of people at high risk from climate-related hazards differs greatly by region
content.subtitle: Population shares defined as being at high-risk from climate-related hazards in 2024
content.visdescription: The visualization shows the share of regional populations defined as being at high risk of climate-related hazards in 2024.
content.textParagraph: On this map, the size of the grey circles is proportional to the size of the population of the seven World Bank regions. The blue circles show the size of the population exposed to climate-related hazards. The red circle shows the size of the population at high risk (both exposed to climate-related hazards and in vulnerable conditions). In North America less than one percent of the population (approximately 0.6 million people) are at high risk. In contrast, more than a third of the people living in the Middle East, North Africa, Afghanistan and Pakistan and Sub-Saharan Africa regions are at high risk.

content.text: A little under a fifth of the global population are at high risk from climate-related hazards. That is, they are both exposed to climate-related hazards and identified as being highly vulnerable. Across countries, the population at high risk can differ widely due to differences in either exposure or vulnerability.

content.title: Population at high risk depends on both exposure and vulnerability
content.subtitle: Comparing countries with similar levels of exposure or vulnerability
content.keyFactLabel: people are both exposed to climate-related hazards and in highly vulnerable conditions

scenes.text: [emphasis: Burkina Faso ]and [emphasis: Kenya ]have similar levels of [emphasis: vulnerability]: in both countries around 9 in 10 people  are identified as vulnerable in at least one dimension.

But the [emphasis: exposure ]to climate hazards is three times higher in Burkina Faso. Combining the number of people vulnerable with those exposed, only 1 in 4 people in Kenya are at risk from climate hazards, compared with 3 in 4 people in Burkina Faso.

In the East Asia and Pacific region, the populations of the [emphasis: Philippines ]and [emphasis: Vietnam] are similarly [emphasis: exposed ]to climate hazards. In both countries almost 9 out of 10 people are exposed.

However, despite similar [emphasis: exposure ]profiles, slightly more than half the people are at risk in the Philippines, while the same is true for around 20 percent of the Vietnamese population. This is due to the differing levels of vulnerability. In the Philippines, 3 out of 5 people are vulnerable, while the same is true for only 1 in 5 people in Vietnam.

content.text: Mitigation, reducing vulnerabilities and building resilience

Climate-related hazards can threaten people’s health, livelihood, food security, and safety, deepening existing inequalities. People who are at high risk are those who are exposed to climate-related hazards such as floods, agricultural droughts, cyclones and heatwaves, and also live in vulnerable conditions. Individually, 4.5 billion people were exposed to climate-related hazards and 2.8 billion lived in vulnerable conditions. Together, this means that 1.5 billion people were at high risk of climate-related hazards in 2024.

To reduce the number of people at high risk, governments need to rely both on climate mitigation as well as adaptation. By taking measures to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and limit future warming, governments can slow the growth of climate-related hazards and exposure to them. Governments work to reduce people’s vulnerabilities and increase their resilience towards climate-related hazards. The World Bank has developed the [emphasis: Adaptation and Resilience Readiness Assessment Tool] to assess how well countries are positioned to design and implement effective climate change adaptation and resilience strategies by evaluating key policies, institutions, and actions across multiple pillars and indicators. [reference: WB1]

This tool, first applied in 17 Caribbean countries, has since guided adaptation efforts elsewhere. In [emphasis: Zambia ]and [emphasis: Brazil], for instance, World Bank–supported programs helped shift from reactive to proactive resilience building, contributing to substantial declines in vulnerability and the number of people at high risk from climate-related hazards. Experiences such as the ones from Zambia and Brazil show that reducing climate risk is possible, even as exposure continues to increase. Continued investments in adaptation, resilience, and inclusive development can further reduce vulnerabilities and protect lives and livelihoods from shocks, including climate‑related shocks.

references.Bloemendaal1: Bloemendaal, N., Haigh, I. D., de Moel, H., Muis, S., Haarsma, R. J., & Aerts, J. C. (2020). Generation of a Global Synthetic Tropical Cyclone Hazard Dataset Using STORM. Scientific data, 7(1), 40. doi.org/10.1038/s41597-020-0381-2
